perturbation.original_text: Terms are net cash upon delivery.
perturbation.changed_text: Terms are net 90 days from delivery.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment terms to 'net 90 days' without considering potential regulations on payment timelines for goods sold in Texas could create conflict. This is especially true if the distributor is considered a 'small business'.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Texas Prompt Payment Act, Texas Government Code, Chapter 2251
perturbation.law_explanation: The Texas Prompt Payment Act sets specific deadlines for payments to small businesses by governmental entities. While this contract isn't directly with a governmental entity, extending payment terms to 90 days could be seen as an attempt to circumvent similar protections if the distributor qualifies as a small business and the product is being indirectly supplied to a governmental entity. It could create a conflict.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: subcontractor if: (1) there is a bona fide dispute between the political subdivision and a vendor, contractor, subcontractor, or supplier about the goods delivered or the service performed that causes the payment to be late; (2) there is a bona fide dispute between a vendor and a subcontractor or between a subcontractor and its supplier about the goods delivered or the service performed that causes the payment to be late; (3) the terms of a federal contract, grant, regulation, or statute prevent the governmental entity from making a timely payment with federal funds; or (4) the invoice is not mailed to the person to whom it is addressed in strict accordance with any instruction on the purchase order relating to the payment. (b) This chapter does not affect Chapter 2253 . (c) Repealed by Acts 2001, 77th Leg., ch. 1158, Sec. 94(4), eff. June 15, 2001. Added by Acts 1993, 73rd Leg., ch. 268, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1993. Amended by Acts 1995, 74th Leg., ch. 76, Sec. 5.41(a), eff. Sept. 1, 1995; Acts 2001, 77th Leg., ch. 1158, Sec. 61, eff. June 15, 2001; Acts 2001, 77th Leg., ch. 1158, Sec. 94(4), eff. June 15, 2001; Acts 2003, 78th Leg., ch. 286, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 2003. Sec. 2251.003. RULES. The comptroller shall establish procedures and adopt rules to administer this chapter. Before adopting a rule under this section, the comptroller must conduct a public hearing regarding the proposed rule regardless of whether the requirements of Section 2001.029 (b) are met. Added by Acts 1993, 73rd Leg., ch. 268, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1993. Amended by Acts 2001, 77th Leg., ch. 1158, Sec. 62, eff. June 15, 2001. Amended by: Acts 2007, 80th Leg., R.S., Ch. 937 (H.B. 3560 ), Sec. 1.73, eff. September 1, 2007. Sec. 2251.004. WAIVER. A person may not waive any right or remedy granted by this chapter. A purported waiver of any right or remedy granted by this chapter is void. Added by Acts 2003, 78th Leg., ch. 286, Sec. 2, eff. Sept. 1, 2003. SUBCHAPTER B. PAYMENTS AND INTEREST Sec. 2251.021. TIME FOR PAYMENT BY GOVERNMENTAL ENTITY. (a) Except as otherwise provided by this section, a payment by a governmental entity under a contract executed on or after September 1, 1987, is overdue on the 31st day after the later of: (1) the date the governmental entity receives the goods under the contract; (2) the date the performance of the service under the contract is completed; or (3) the date the governmental entity receives an invoice for the goods or service. (b) A payment under a contract executed on or after September 1, 1993, owed by a political subdivision whose governing body meets only once a month or less frequently is overdue on the 46th day after the later event described by Subsections (a)(1) through (3). (b-1) A payment under a contract for legal services described by Section 402.0212 owed by a state agency is overdue on the 46th day after the date the agency receives an invoice for the legal services. (c) For a contract executed on or after July 1, 1986, and before September 1, 1987, a payment by a governmental entity under that contract is overdue on the 46th day after the later event described by Subsections (a)(1) through (3). (d) For purposes of this section, the renewal, amendment, or extension of a contract executed on or before September 1, 1993, is considered to be the execution of a new contract. Added by Acts 1993, 73rd Leg., ch. 268, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1993. Amended by Acts 1995, 74th Leg., ch. 76, Sec. 5.42(a), eff. Sept. 1, 1995; Acts 2001, 77th Leg., ch. 1158, Sec. 63, eff. June 15, 2001. Amended by: Acts 2019, 86th Leg., R.S., Ch. 399 (S.B. 1370 ), Sec. 2, eff. September 1, 2019. Sec. 2251.022. TIME FOR PAYMENT BY VENDOR. (a) A vendor who receives a payment from a governmental entity shall pay a subcontractor the appropriate share of the payment not later than the 10th day after the date the vendor receives the payment. (b) The appropriate share is overdue on the 11th day after the date the vendor receives the payment. Added by A
perturbation.justification: The modification changes the payment terms from 'net cash upon delivery' to 'net 90 days from delivery.' The Law Explanation suggests this could conflict with the Texas Prompt Payment Act, especially if the distributor is a small business indirectly supplying to a government entity. Scraped Law Snippet 1, Section 2251.021(a), states that a payment by a governmental entity is overdue on the 31st day after the later of receiving goods, completing service, or receiving an invoice. Extending payment terms to 90 days directly contradicts this provision, creating an inconsistency regarding legally mandated payment timelines. The modification therefore establishes a clear inconsistency legal contradiction, as it could violate state law regarding payment deadlines.

perturbation.original_text: Company further warrants all Products to be free from defects in material or workmanship under normal use and service for a period of ninety (90) days from the date of delivery.
perturbation.changed_text: Company further warrants all Products to be free from defects in material or workmanship under normal use and service for a period of thirty (30) days from the date of delivery.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the warranty period to 30 days may conflict with implied warranty laws, particularly the implied warranty of merchantability, depending on the nature of the product and reasonable consumer expectations in South America (the territory defined in the contract).
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code § 2-314. Implied Warranty: Merchantability; Usage of Trade.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the UCC is a US law, the concept of implied warranty exists in most jurisdictions, including those in South America, although the specifics vary. Reducing the warranty period to 30 days may be considered unreasonably short for a product like a nasal aspirator, potentially violating the implied warranty of merchantability, which requires goods to be fit for their ordinary purpose. The contract states that Texas law governs the agreement, and thus the UCC is applicable. A 30-day warranty may not be considered commercially reasonable.

perturbation.justification: The modification reduces the warranty period from 90 to 30 days, potentially violating the implied warranty of merchantability. The Law Explanation argues a 30-day warranty may not be commercially reasonable, and the Perturbation Explanation notes the conflict with implied warranty laws. Since the contract states that Texas law governs the agreement, and thus the UCC is applicable, reducing the warranty period to 30 days introduces an inconsistency relative to typical commercial expectations, hence a 'YES'.

perturbation.original_text: Any such notice or communication so mailed shall be deemed delivered and effective seventy-two (72) hours after mailing thereof in the United States.
perturbation.changed_text: Any such notice or communication so mailed shall be deemed delivered and effective fourteen (14) days after mailing thereof in the United States.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the notice period to 14 days is unreasonable, especially given that South America is defined as the territory. This extended timeframe for notice could create issues with fulfilling contractual obligations in a timely manner, potentially conflicting with good faith and fair dealing requirements under contract law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under UCC 1-304, 'Every contract or duty within [the Uniform Commercial Code] imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.' Imposing a 14-day window for notice to be effective to a distributor in South America is not commercially reasonable, given the distance, customs and language differences, and the Texas choice of law. This could lead to claims that the Company is not acting in good faith.

perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the notice period to 14 days for a distributor in South America. The Law Explanation states that this is not commercially reasonable. This creates an inconsistency because the extended timeframe could conflict with good faith requirements, violating UCC 1-304, as described in the Law Explanation. The Perturbation Explanation highlights that this extended timeframe could create issues with fulfilling contractual obligations in a timely manner.
